name: US DoD — Chief Digital & AI Office (CDAO)
overview: The CDAO is the Pentagon's central office for digital and AI, set up in 2022 by merging four older data and AI bodies under one chief reporting near the top of the Department. Its public footprint is unusually concrete for a government AI office: it owns the responsible-AI rulebook and toolkit, runs the military's flagship AI targeting platform (the Palantir-built Maven Smart System), delivered a first working version of the 'connect-everything' battle network (CJADC2), and now drives a generative-AI fielding effort. It mostly coordinates and accelerates rather than building everything itself, leaning heavily on commercial suppliers.

established.label: The Pentagon's AI headquarters
established.plain: In February 2022 the US military created one senior office to run all its digital and AI work, instead of leaving it scattered. It was built by folding together four existing teams — the data office, the Joint AI Center, the Defense Digital Service and an analytics office — and the new chief reports close to the top of the Department. Think of it as the front door and the conductor for military AI.

established.label: The rulebook for doing AI responsibly
established.plain: In June 2022 the CDAO published the official plan for using AI ethically across the military — built on five principles (responsible, equitable, traceable, reliable, governable). It is not a product but the guardrails: it lays out the work needed so AI systems are accountable and trustworthy before they are used.

established.label: The flagship AI targeting platform
established.plain: In May 2024 the CDAO awarded Palantir a five-year deal worth up to $480 million to expand the Maven Smart System — software that pulls together satellite imagery, sensor feeds and other data to help spot potential targets and speed up decisions. The award took the user base 'from hundreds to thousands' across commands worldwide. This is the most operationally real piece of the CDAO's work.

established.label: A practical checklist for AI teams
established.plain: In November 2023 the CDAO released a free toolkit that turns the responsible-AI principles into a hands-on process. Teams use it voluntarily to check, track and improve how well their AI projects line up with the ethical standards. It is the bridge from policy on paper to something a project team can actually follow.

established.label: First working version of the all-service battle network
established.plain: In February 2024 the CDAO delivered a first usable version of CJADC2 — the long-running goal of connecting sensors, data and commanders across all services and domains into one network so decisions can be made faster. Officials called it 'real and ready now'. It is an early build, not the finished vision, but it proved the concept works in the field.

inProgress.label: The fast lane for generative AI
inProgress.plain: Launched in December 2024 and run by the CDAO with the Defense Innovation Unit, this cell is meant to get generative-AI tools into use quickly, with about $100 million behind it. That includes roughly $35M for four fast 'frontier AI' pilots and about $40M in small-business contracts. It is the permanent successor to the earlier Task Force Lima.

inProgress.claim: CDAO support to the Replicator initiative
inProgress.label: Helping the drone-swarm push with data
inProgress.plain: Replicator is the Pentagon's drive to field thousands of cheap autonomous systems, led mainly by others. The CDAO's job is the data and AI plumbing behind it — officials said they are looking at an 'AI data hub' to share labelled data and lessons across the many companies and units involved, so the autonomy learns faster. The hub's timeline was not made public.

inProgress.claim: Maven Smart System → program of record
inProgress.label: Making the targeting platform permanent and bigger
inProgress.plain: A March 2026 directive from the deputy defense secretary orders the Maven Smart System moved onto a permanent funding footing (a 'program of record') by the end of the fiscal year, with system management shifting to a dedicated CDAO program office. The stated aim is stable money so the AI-targeting tool can be embedded right across military operations — set as the cornerstone for CJADC2.

latest.label: Spending on the AI platform surges
latest.plain: Driven by 'growing demand', the Pentagon increased the Palantir Maven Smart System contract in May 2025, lifting the total agreement to roughly $1.3 billion — well above the original $480 million ceiling. It is a clear signal of how central this AI platform has become and how fast its use is spreading.

latest.label: A hard deadline to roll it out everywhere
latest.plain: By April 2026, reporting showed military components facing a demanding schedule to transition onto the Maven Smart System as a department-wide standard. It underlines that the CDAO's push is now less about experimenting and more about getting one AI platform adopted across the whole force, quickly.

suppliers.name: Palantir Technologies
suppliers.role: Builds and runs the Maven Smart System AI targeting platform; five-year award up to $480M (2024), later raised toward $1.3B (2025)
suppliers.claim: The NGA Maven program, arguably the most successful and sought-after AI program across the defense and intelligence communities. — Shyam Sankar, Palantir CTO, to Congress

suppliers.name: Defense Innovation Unit (DIU)
suppliers.role: Co-leads the AI Rapid Capabilities Cell with the CDAO to field generative AI fast (DoD partner organisation, not a commercial vendor)
suppliers.claim: Our partnership with CDAO, and collaboration on the Rapid Capabilities Cell, will allow us to shape critical AI initiatives… from the beginning. — Doug Beck, DIU Director

suppliers.name: Google, Oracle, AWS, Microsoft (JWCC cloud providers)
suppliers.role: Provide the cloud compute and 'sandboxes' the AI Rapid Capabilities Cell uses for generative-AI experiments

sources.name: Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Office (Wikipedia overview)
Pentagon creates new CDAO overseer — Breaking Defense
AI Rapid Capabilities Cell launch — DefenseScoop

unknowns: The internal results of CDAO programmes — what generative-AI pilots actually delivered, the real-world performance of Maven targeting, and the design and timing of the Replicator AI data hub — are largely not public. Org charts and leadership have also shifted with the change of administration, so current internal structure may differ from what published sources describe.
